evidence.quote_or_summary: The best time to be on this trek is from the beginning of July to the middle of September. That's the only time you can trek here.
In all the other seasons, Jammu & Kashmir trails are buried under snow.

values: Early July: snow remains at higher altitudes, trek accessible, colourful flowers appearing on meadows, lush green meadows, alpine lakes still frozen in parts, light evening showers
Mid-July to third week of August: most snow melted, frozen ice in lakes melted, bright sunny mornings with some evening showers, green meadows densely dotted with wildflowers
Aug-end to second week of September: cold sets in, rains reduce, brilliant blue skies, deep blue alpine lakes, meadows change colour from fresh green to a different tone, flowers change

evidence.quote_or_summary: Bodpathri in early July ... Snow remains at higher altitudes. But the trek is more or less accessible. As the snow melts, colourful flowers start appearing on the meadows.

Bodpathri from mid-July to third week of August ... Most snow has melted by now. Frozen chunks of ice in most lakes on the trail would have melted. ... The trail is carpeted with green meadows everywhere. The best part is, that these meadows are densely dotted with wildflowers!

Bodpathri from Aug-end to second week of September ... The clouds part during this time of the year and you witness brilliant blue skies. Their reflection deepens the blue of the alpine lakes. ... The grass no longer retains the soft, fresh green tinge. The flowers change.

values: Snow remains at higher altitudes in early July; most snow melted by mid-July to third week of August; snowfall can be expected during the second week of September

evidence.quote_or_summary: Snow remains at higher altitudes. But the trek is more or less accessible.
Most snow has melted by now. Frozen chunks of ice in most lakes on the trail would have melted.
You can expect snowfall during the second week of September

values: Light evening showers in early July; some evening showers mid-July to third week of August; rains reduce after end of August; Pir Panjal range blocks most rain clouds so rains are mild compared to Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand

evidence.quote_or_summary: It's warm and you experience light evening showers during this month.
Expect bright sunny mornings with some evening showers. Rains are still mild compared to rains in Himachal Pradesh and Uttarakhand.
After the end of August, cold sets in. Rains reduce giving way to one of the best times to do the trek.
This range is 288 km long and 40-50 km wide. It blocks most rain clouds from crossing over into the Kashmir Valley.

values: Early July: daytime 15-20°C, nighttime 2-7°C; Mid-July to third week of August: daytime 15-20°C, nighttime 4-8°C; Aug-end to second week of September: daytime 14-18°C, nighttime 0-5°C

evidence.quote_or_summary: Day time temperature: Between 15 °C and 20 °C | Night time temperature : Between 2 °C and 7 °C
Day time temperature: Between 15 °C and 20 °C | Night time temperature : Between 4 °C and 8 °C
Day time temperature: Between 14 °C and 18 °C | Night time temperature : Between 0 °C and 5 °C

values: Lush green meadows punctuated with alpine lakes still frozen in parts (early July); meadows densely dotted with wildflowers (mid-July to third week of August); brilliant blue skies reflecting in deep blue alpine lakes, meadows change colour from fresh green to a different tone, flowers change (Aug-end to second week of September)

evidence.quote_or_summary: The landscape has lush green meadows punctuated with alpine lakes that are still frozen in parts.
The trail is carpeted with green meadows everywhere. The best part is, that these meadows are densely dotted with wildflowers!
The clouds part during this time of the year and you witness brilliant blue skies. Their reflection deepens the blue of the alpine lakes.

values: Evening showers may occur; cross the pass early and reach next campsite by 4 PM; snowfall chances increase after second week of September making the pass uncrossable and the trail on the other side inaccessible; trek is usually stopped after the second week of September

evidence.quote_or_summary: You might experience evening showers on certain days during this time of the year. This is why it is a good practice to cross the pass early in the day and reach the next campsite by 4 PM.

We usually stop our trek after the second week of September. It turns colder and chances of snowfall increase. That raises the difficulty of the trek and it's no longer possible to cross the pass. The trail on the other side becomes inaccessible.

values: Trekkers seeking a short monsoon-season window with mild rains, wildflower meadows, and changing alpine landscapes in Jammu & Kashmir

evidence.quote_or_summary: July to September happens to be peak summer transitioning to Autumn and the best time to trek in Jammu & Kashmir. The landscape comes alive during this window.

values: Trekkers looking for snow trekking; trekking outside the July to mid-September window as trails are buried under snow

evidence.quote_or_summary: In all the other seasons, Jammu & Kashmir trails are buried under snow.
